title: Francis Fergus O’Farrell

infobox.value: * [Franco-Dutch War](/wiki/Franco-Dutch_War "Franco-Dutch War") * [Glorious Revolution 1688](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution_1688 "Glorious Revolution 1688") * [Nine Years' War](/wiki/Nine_Years%27_War "Nine Years' War")   + [Walcourt](/wiki/Battle_of_Walcourt "Battle of Walcourt")   + [Battle of Steenkerque](/wiki/Battle_of_Steenkerque "Battle of Steenkerque")   + [Landen](/wiki/Battle_of_Landen "Battle of Landen")   + [Deinze](/wiki/Capitulation_of_Diksmuide "Capitulation of Diksmuide") * [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession")
**Colonel of [The Royal Scots Fusiliers](/wiki/The_Royal_Scots_Fusiliers "The Royal Scots Fusiliers")**  1689–1695 | Succeeded by Robert Mackay

c. 1650 to 1712, was an Irish-born professional soldier who served in the [Dutch States Army](/wiki/Dutch_States_Army "Dutch States Army") under [William III](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England") until 1689, when he transferred into the [English Army](/wiki/English_Army "English Army"). During the [Nine Years' War](/wiki/Nine_Years%27_War "Nine Years' War") in 1695, he was cashiered for his premature surrender of the town of [Deinze](/wiki/Deinze "Deinze"), then reinstated in 1696 and served in the [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession").

infobox.key: Colonel of [The Royal Scots Fusiliers](/wiki/The_Royal_Scots_Fusiliers "The Royal Scots Fusiliers")

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Francis Fergus O’Farrell](/w/index.php?title=Francis_Fergus_O%E2%80%99Farrell&redirect=no "Francis Fergus O’Farrell"))

Not to be confused with [Frank O'Farrell](/wiki/Frank_O%27Farrell "Frank O'Farrell") or [Francis O'Farrell](/wiki/Francis_O%27Farrell "Francis O'Farrell").

| Francis Fergus O'Farrell | |
| --- | --- |
| [Deinze](/wiki/Deinze "Deinze"); O'Farrell's surrender of this town in 1695 resulted in his court-martial | |
| Born | c. 1650  [County Longford](/wiki/County_Longford "County Longford"), [Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") |
| Died | 1712 (aged 54–55) |
| Branch | Infantry |
| Rank | Major-General |
| Conflicts | * [Franco-Dutch War](/wiki/Franco-Dutch_War "Franco-Dutch War") * [Glorious Revolution 1688](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution_1688 "Glorious Revolution 1688") * [Nine Years' War](/wiki/Nine_Years%27_War "Nine Years' War")   + [Walcourt](/wiki/Battle_of_Walcourt "Battle of Walcourt")   + [Battle of Steenkerque](/wiki/Battle_of_Steenkerque "Battle of Steenkerque")   + [Landen](/wiki/Battle_of_Landen "Battle of Landen")   + [Deinze](/wiki/Capitulation_of_Diksmuide "Capitulation of Diksmuide") * [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession") |

[Major General](/wiki/Major_General "Major General") **Francis Fergus O’Farrell** c. 1650 to 1712, was an Irish-born professional soldier who served in the [Dutch States Army](/wiki/Dutch_States_Army "Dutch States Army") under [William III](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England") until 1689, when he transferred into the [English Army](/wiki/English_Army "English Army"). During the [Nine Years' War](/wiki/Nine_Years%27_War "Nine Years' War") in 1695, he was cashiered for his premature surrender of the town of [Deinze](/wiki/Deinze "Deinze"), then reinstated in 1696 and served in the [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession").

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Francis_Fergus_O%27Farrell&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Francis Fergus O’Farrell was born in [County Longford](/wiki/County_Longford "County Longford") in 1650, presumably a member of the [O’Farrell](/wiki/%C3%93_Fearghail "Ó Fearghail") clan who traditionally dominated the area, although his parentage is unclear. His distant cousin Ceadagh O'Farrell was killed at the [Battle of the Boyne](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Boyne "Battle of the Boyne") in 1690 fighting for the [Jacobites](/wiki/Jacobitism "Jacobitism"), while other relatives joined the French [Irish Brigade](/wiki/Irish_Brigade_(France) "Irish Brigade (France)"), so Francis was unusual in serving the [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") [William of Orange](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England").

O'Farrell had at least two children, a son also named Francis Fergus who married Mary Emmett in June 1712, and a daughter Diana (?–before 1728), who married [Francis Howard, 1st Earl of Effingham](/wiki/Francis_Howard,_1st_Earl_of_Effingham "Francis Howard, 1st Earl of Effingham") in February 1713.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDoyle1885661-1)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Francis_Fergus_O%27Farrell&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

O’Farrell appears to have begun his military career serving in one of the regiments of the [Scots Brigade](/wiki/Scots_Brigade "Scots Brigade") and took part in the 1672 to 1678 [Franco-Dutch War](/wiki/Franco-Dutch_War "Franco-Dutch War"). By 1688, he had reached the rank of [Lieutenant Colonel](/wiki/Lieutenant_Colonel "Lieutenant Colonel") and was part of the invasion force that accompanied William of Orange to England in the November 1688 [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution"). There was very little fighting; the vast majority of [James II](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England")'s army simply changed sides and he went into exile in France. On 1 March 1689, O'Farrell was commissioned Colonel of the [Earl of Mars Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Scots_Fusiliers "Royal Scots Fusiliers") in place of [Thomas Buchan](/wiki/Thomas_Buchan "Thomas Buchan"), a Scottish Catholic who had remained loyal to James. It reached [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders") in time to fight at the [Battle of Walcourt](/wiki/Battle_of_Walcourt "Battle of Walcourt") in August 1689 and is listed in the 1691 return as a "[Fusilier](/wiki/Fusilier "Fusilier")" regiment, a designation reserved for elite units.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECannon18495-2)

O'Farrell was captured by a French patrol in July 1692 [[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds1991194-3) but was quickly released since he was present at the [Battle of Steenkerque](/wiki/Battle_of_Steenkerque "Battle of Steenkerque") in August and also fought at [Landen](/wiki/Battle_of_Landen "Battle of Landen") in 1693. However, in July 1695 as commander of the garrison at [Deinze](/wiki/Deinze "Deinze") he surrendered it to the French without resistance, the town of [Diksmuide](/wiki/Capitulation_of_Diksmuide "Capitulation of Diksmuide") doing the same. Prisoners were normally exchanged as soon as possible but the French retained the 6,000 - 7,000 troops captured at Diksmuide and Deinze due to a dispute over the terms of their surrender. Shortage of manpower was becoming an issue and the French used a various methods to encourage prisoners to enlist in their army, including moving officers and men into prison cells; O'Farrell wrote to a friend on 5 September complaining of this treatment.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChilds199140-4)

William dealt harshly with the officers from Deinze and Diksmuide; Ellenberg was executed in Ghent on 30 November, O'Farrell dismissed and those who signed the surrender documents court-martialled.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWalton1894304-5) Most were quickly reinstated, including O'Farrell who was reappointed in 1696, although he lost command of his regiment.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalton190488-6) During the [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession") in January 1704, he was promoted [Major General](/wiki/Major_General "Major General"), [[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDalton190617-7) and according to [Parliamentary](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain") records was one of a number of general officers assigned to the army of [John V of Portugal](/wiki/John_V_of_Portugal "John V of Portugal"), a position he retained until December 1708.[[8]](#cite_note-8) He died in 1712.
